narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and an LSAT score of 40—both above average for typical students—your law school application stood out in several ways, including your high Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 9 and your upper-middle family income background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 59.6% chance that you would fail it, tipping the decision toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor bar passage outcomes for the program. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the outcome. The most influential was your undergraduate GPA; while it's respectable and slightly above the average of 3.21, it still pushed the model toward predicting failure because GPAs in this range have historically correlated with higher bar failure rates compared to top performers. Next, your Year 3 school decile of 9 worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it shows you ranked in the top 10% of your peers that year—better than the average decile of about 5.5—and this nearly offset the GPA concern. Your LSAT score came third in influence, with its above-average strength of 40 (versus the typical 36.7) nudging things slightly toward passing, though not enough to overcome the leading factor. Overall, your full-time student status, common among 93% of applicants, didn't sway things much, but the balance tipped against you because the model weighs academic history heavily for bar success. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile is competitive in test scores and later-year performance but falls short in the GPA area that admissions prioritizes most for this risk assessment. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push it even higher or strengthening your GPA narrative with explanations or additional coursework, then reapply to programs with more flexible thresholds. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early, as students like you with strong late momentum often outperform predictions with focused effort. This rejection isn't a reflection of your potential as an Asian male applicant—many in similar positions succeed by addressing these exact gaps. Keep building on those high deciles, and targeted improvements could flip the script next time.
